prompts.format-focus:
Extract and transcribe all text from this typewritten document.
Preserve the original formatting:
- Keep line breaks where they appear
- Maintain paragraph spacing
- Preserve indentation and alignment
Output only the transcribed text.
prompts.optimized-v1:
You are an OCR system. TRANSCRIBE this typewritten document exactly.

Read every word from top to bottom, left to right.
Copy ALL text - titles, paragraphs, lists, signatures, footnotes.

Preserve formatting:
- Keep line breaks where they appear
- Maintain paragraph spacing
- Preserve indentation

Output ONLY the transcribed text.
prompts.anti-title-only:
This document has MULTIPLE paragraphs of text - not just a title.
TRANSCRIBE the complete document from first line to last line.
Do NOT stop after the heading. Keep reading until you reach the bottom of the page.
Output every word exactly as written.
prompts.continue-reading:
Read and transcribe this ENTIRE page of text.
The document contains many lines - continue reading past the title.
Start at the top and do not stop until you reach the very last line.
Output all text exactly as written, preserving line breaks.
prompts.many-lines:
This is a full page of typewritten text with 20+ lines.
Transcribe EVERY line from top to bottom:
- Line 1 (usually a title or heading)
- Lines 2-10 (body paragraphs)
- Lines 11-20+ (more content, possibly lists or signatures)
Output the complete transcription.
prompts.raw-text-only:
OUTPUT ONLY THE WORDS WRITTEN ON THIS PAGE.

Do not:
- Describe what you see
- Summarize the content
- Say "This document shows..." or "The text reads..."
- Add any commentary

Just output the raw text, line by line, exactly as written.
prompts.copyist:
You are a copyist. Your job is to copy text exactly.
Look at each word on this page and write it down.
Do not interpret, summarize, or describe.
Copy every word from top to bottom, preserving the layout.
prompts.strict-accuracy:
Transcribe ONLY what is actually written on this page.
Do not guess, infer, or make up any words.
If a word is unclear, transcribe your best reading of it.
Never add content that is not visible in the image.
Output the exact text, nothing more.
prompts.optimized-v2:
TRANSCRIBE this entire typewritten page from first line to last.

IMPORTANT:
- This document has MANY paragraphs - do not stop after the title
- Output raw text only - no descriptions or summaries
- Copy exactly what is written - do not add or invent content

Read from top to bottom. Preserve line breaks and formatting.
Output every word on the page.
prompts.dual-instruction:
Task 1: Read every line of text on this page, from the title at the top to the last line at the bottom.

Task 2: Output exactly what you read. No descriptions, no summaries, just the raw text.

Begin transcription:
prompts.letter-aware:
This is a typewritten document (possibly a letter, memo, or manuscript).
Transcribe the complete text including:
- Date and header (if present)
- Salutation or title
- All body paragraphs
- Closing and signature
- Any postscripts or footnotes

Output the full transcription, preserving formatting.
prompts.verbose:
You are performing OCR (Optical Character Recognition) on a typewritten document.

Your task:
1. Start at the very top of the page
2. Read the first line (this is often a title or heading - include it)
3. Continue reading line by line
4. Do NOT stop after the first few lines - there is more text below
5. Keep reading until you reach the absolute bottom of the page
6. Output everything you read, preserving the original line breaks

Critical rules:
- Output ONLY the transcribed text
- Never describe the document
- Never summarize the content
- Include every single line of text

Begin:

details.actual: The clock on the mantle she had been empty for eleven years withdrawn from the door, she finally sat at the counter, the feet, and the wall.
details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:
The morning train
The late form empty, still and grey,
Before the crowd arrives
A distanced lookout like silver knives
Cuts the morning air
The engine rounds the final bend
All steam and smoke and glare
The door slide open, people rush
And I stand on, watch and find my seat
And watch the world outside
* * * *
The city shinks behind the glass
The field rolls into view
And some where in this rocking car
I start my life anew
details.expected:
THE MORNING TRAIN

	The platform empty, still, and grey,
	Before the crowd arrives --
	I stand alone and watch the rails
	Stretch out like silver knives.

	A distant whistle, low and long,
	Cuts through the morning air.
	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,
	A hundred lives collide.
	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

details.actual: October 15, 1962 Dear Margaret, I hope this letter finds you well. It has been far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself thinking of our conversation often. The autumn has been particularly beautiful this year. The mages along the drive have turned brilliant shades of orange, and the garden is still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights. I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned about the lighthouse keeper. I have been unable to find you at our local shop, and wondered if you might send me your name. With warm regards, Margaret. P.S. Do give my best to your mother.
details.expected:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

	I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me the author's name.

		With warm regards,
		Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.

details.expected:
March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

details.actual: How to Develop Black and White Film.
details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

details.actual: How to Develop Black and White Film.
details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

details.actual: The clock on the mantle she finally sat at.
details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:
The morning train
The late form empty, still, and grey,
Before the crowd arrives
-- I stand alone and watch the rails
Stretch
A distant whistle, low and long,
Cuts the morning air
The engine rounds the final bend
All steam and smoke and glare
The door slide open, people rush
And I start on, find my seat,
And watch the world outside
* * * * *

unable to find you at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me your name.

details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual: The morning train.
details.expected:
THE MORNING TRAIN

	The platform empty, still, and grey,
	Before the crowd arrives --
	I stand alone and watch the rails
	Stretch out like silver knives.

	A distant whistle, low and long,
	Cuts through the morning air.
	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,
	A hundred lives collide.
	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:
The morning train
The late form empty, still, and grey,
Before the crowd arrives
A distanced lookout like silver knives
Cuts the morning air
The engine rounds the final bend
All steam and smoke and glare
The door slide open, and rush
And I see the seat on watch, and find my seat
And watch the world outside
* * *
details.expected:
THE MORNING TRAIN

	The platform empty, still, and grey,
	Before the crowd arrives --
	I stand alone and watch the rails
	Stretch out like silver knives.

	A distant whistle, low and long,
	Cuts through the morning air.
	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,
	A hundred lives collide.
	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.actual: A Brief History of Photography.
details.expected:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

I.   Early Experiments (1820-1840)
     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph
     C. Daguerre's contributions
	  1. The daguerreotype process
	  2. Commercial applications

II.  The Wet Plate Era (1851-1880)
     A. Collodion process
     B. Advantages over daguerreotype
	  - Shorter exposure times
	  - Reproducible negatives
     C. Notable practitioners
	  1. Mathew Brady
	  2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak Revolution (1888-1920)
     A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
     B. Roll film development
     C. Amateur photography emerges

IV.  Questions for Discussion
     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is

March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.
